Jin was sixteen years old when she started working at her father's restaurant. Her father had opened it when he was twenty-one, after his own parents helped him start the business. He had left Greece as a teenager and worked in restaurants owned by other immigrants. Now he owned his own place. He was proud of it. And he expected Jin to take over one day.

But Jin had no interest in running a restaurant. She wanted to do something that felt meaningful. Something that fulfilled a sense of purpose. When she worked at the restaurant as a teenager, she would sometimes lock herself in the bathroom and cry. She felt trapped. Overwhelmed. She did not want to spend her life running a restaurant.

Throughout high school, Jin desperately tried to find other jobs during summer breaks. She did not want to run the register or wait tables. Her father eventually realized that Jin intended to live her life on her own terms. He understood why it was important for her to pursue her own dreams, because he had made the same decision despite what had been expected of him. He could see himself reflected in her.

But Jin's mother had different expectations. She had unrealistically high standards. Jin felt immense pressure to make her mother proud. This meant being involved in every possible extracurricular activity while also maintaining an excellent academic record. When Jin graduated third in her high school class, her mother expressed disappointment that Jin did not make salutatorian or valedictorian. No matter what degree of success Jin achieved, she felt like she could never do or be enough.

Jin had always been a very creative child. She loved reading, writing, and creating art. She tried to funnel her artistic passions into a profession her mother would approve of. Architecture seemed like a good compromise. It was creative but also respectable and financially lucrative. Her mother was supportive of architecture.

But after the suicide of a close friend, Jin started to think about pursuing a career as a therapist instead. She wanted to help people. To cultivate meaningful human connection. When she started talking about her interest in the social sciences, her mother perceived that as a downgrade. She questioned why Jin would be willing to give up security and stability.

Jin felt obligated to please her mother, especially after rejecting her father's business. So she applied to a school with one of the best architecture programs in the country and was accepted. But she could not stop thinking about pursuing a career that would allow her to help people. Just a few days before the start of her freshman year, Jin filed the paperwork to change her areas of study to sociology and psychology.

She did not tell her parents immediately. She was afraid of their disappointment. She knew they wanted her to choose a safe path that would lead to guaranteed success. They pushed her hard and expected a lot from her because they did not want her to struggle in the same ways they had. They just wanted what was best for her, but they unintentionally stifled her spirit in the process.

As children of immigrants, Jin and others like her wanted to make their parents proud. They wanted their parents to feel that their sacrifice was worth whatever they might have given up. But what did that mean, really? Did they carry on their parents' legacy, or did they create their own? The dilemma was usually further complicated by cultural differences.

Most immigrant parents came from regions with collectivist cultures. These were societies that placed an emphasis on community. Inheriting a family business was the expected norm. The pursuit of individual dreams was not encouraged. But their children found themselves in the United States, a highly individualistic culture that emphasized choice, freedom, and personal fulfillment. They were torn between who they wanted to be and who they were expected to be.

Jin made the decision to pave her own path because she would have become resentful and miserable if she had forced herself to take over her father's business out of a sense of expectation or obligation. She often worried that her parents thought she did not appreciate them because she chose her desires over theirs. But nothing could be further from the truth.

Maybe choosing her own dreams was the ultimate expression of her gratitude. She had a choice because of the sacrifices they had made for her. The ability to define her future was possible because they had made it so. Was she really supposed to follow in their footsteps when everything they did was to make sure she could make choices for herself?

Jin's parents had come to the United States with almost nothing. Her father had left Greece as a teenager. Her mother had grown up in poverty, working on farms with her parents. They had both worked twelve to fourteen hours a day toward the better life they had dreamed of. They wanted Jin to have opportunities they never had. But they also wanted her to choose safe paths. Doctor. Lawyer. Engineer. These were the careers they understood. These were the careers that guaranteed success.

But Jin had no idea what people actually did in an office. She had never been exposed to creative careers. She only knew about the careers with prescribed, laid out paths. Law. Medicine. Engineering. Architecture. These were the careers her parents and their friends understood. These were the careers that seemed safe.

When Jin started college, she met other first-generation students. They talked about their families. About the pressure to succeed. About the guilt they felt over having luxuries their parents were never given. About the constant unspoken pressure to perform the best in classes and academics because of their parents' sacrifices. It could be suffocating at times.

One friend told Jin about how her mother had looked at her very seriously after she earned a master's degree and said, "You know, it's not too late to become a doctor." The friend had started laughing and then burst into tears because she realized her mother was completely serious. No matter what achievement, it was never enough.

Another friend told Jin about how her father had said, "I didn't think you'd be so ordinary," when she chose a career in tech instead of medicine. Those words were painful to hear. They made her feel like she had disappointed her parents and that all the sacrifices they made were not worth it.

Another friend had studied communications, and her father told her she would end up homeless. He wanted her to be an engineer, a doctor, or a dentist. But she had followed her passion instead. She had moved abroad to study, and that experience was the first time she proved to herself she could survive outside her family unit.

Jin listened to these stories and realized she was not alone. Many children of immigrants faced the same struggle. They wanted to honor their parents' sacrifices. But they also wanted to follow their own dreams. They wanted to choose paths that brought them joy, not just security.

During her undergraduate studies, Jin lived in housing designed to cultivate cross-cultural relationships between international and domestic students. This experience had a profound impact on her. It shifted her professional path. She went on to get her master's degree in cultural anthropology and dedicated herself to a career in intercultural education.

She now traveled the world full-time as a location-independent intercultural consultant and coach. She spent every day doing work that gave her purpose. Not everyone, including her parents, was able to comprehend the value of her work or the life she had created for herself. But Jin did. And she had come to realize that had to be enough for her.

It was not her responsibility to mold who she was to fit into a box built out of someone else's expectations. It was not her job to fulfill someone else's dreams. She was here to live out her purpose, and that was what she intended to do.

Jin's personal journey as the daughter of immigrants had taught her a critical life lesson. Only she could give herself the approval and worth she so desperately sought from others. She chose a life that would bring her joy. She did not end up pursuing therapy in the long term, but she found her way to work that helped people understand each other across cultures.

When Jin went home to visit her parents, she would sometimes translate for her father when he needed help understanding something in English. She saw how hard he worked. How much he had sacrificed. How much he wanted her to succeed. But she also saw that he understood, in his own way, why she needed to choose her own path. Because he had done the same thing.

Her mother was slower to come around. But one day, when Jin told her mother about a success in her work, her mother said something Jin had never heard before. "I'm so proud of you." Those words meant everything. Because Jin had spent her entire life trying to hear them.

Jin realized that her parents' sacrifices were made in order to create space for her happiness. Shouldn't she be the one to decide what that happiness looked like? They had given up everything. Their language. Their home. Their culture. All for her. So that she could have choices. So that she could make decisions for herself.

Choosing her own dreams was not a rejection of their sacrifices. It was the ultimate expression of her gratitude. Because she was using the freedom they had given her. She was using the opportunities they had created. She was living the life they had dreamed of for her. Just not in the way they had imagined.

Jin was not following in their footsteps. She was creating her own path. But she was doing it because of everything they had done for her. Their sacrifices had made her choices possible. And she was choosing joy. She was choosing purpose. She was choosing to honor both her immigrant identity and her personal dreams.

She was bridging two worlds. The world of her parents' traditions and expectations. And the world of her own dreams and passions. She did not have to choose one or the other. She could honor both. She could be grateful for their sacrifices while also following her own heart. She could be the daughter they raised while also being the person she was meant to become.

That was the bridge. That was the balance. That was the way forward.
